planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic woodland soil in partial to full shade; western trillium is the Pacific Northwest counterpart to white trillium — purchase nursery-propagated divisions only, never wild-dug plants.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic woodland soil in partial to full shade; western trillium requires the same conditions as eastern white trillium (T. grandiflorum) — cool, moist, shaded conditions and years of patience before it establishes and flowers reliably. Trillium ovatum is the dominant trillium species of Pacific Northwest old-growth forests, producing the classic trillium form of three broad leaves and a single, three-petaled, white flower in early spring that ages to pink and finally purple-red over its two-to-three-week bloom period; it differs from eastern T. grandiflorum primarily in its narrower, more pointed petals. Like all trilliums, it requires 5–7 years from seed to first bloom; divisions from nurseries are the only ethical source — wild collection is destructive and depletes natural populations. Outstanding for shaded native plant gardens in the Pacific Northwest and similar climates.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; largeflower phlox is a tall, shade-adapted native that bridges the gap between sun-loving tall phlox and deep-shade woodland plants.

directions: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; largeflower phlox is more shade-tolerant than garden phlox (P. paniculata) and makes an outstanding tall shade garden perennial at the woodland edge. Phlox amplifolia is a native eastern North American phlox of moist woodland borders and river bottomlands with unusually large, broad leaves (giving it the species name "large-leaved") and tall stems bearing large, fragrant, lavender to pale-purple flowers in summer — blooming later than most native phloxes and filling a valuable midsummer gap in the shade garden. It has better resistance to powdery mildew than P. paniculata in humid climates, and the larger, broader leaves create a bolder textural effect; use at the back of shaded borders alongside native grasses, Joe-Pye weed, and elderberry.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 6–8 inches deep in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; pheasant's eye narcissus is the most shade-tolerant daffodil species and naturalizes beautifully under deciduous trees.

directions: Plant bulbs 6–8 inches deep in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade beneath deciduous trees — the bulbs receive full sun in spring before leaves emerge, and the summer shade helps keep them cool and dry during dormancy. Narcissus poeticus is the wild narcissus of southern European mountain meadows, the most fragrant of all daffodil species, with pure-white petals and a tiny, flat, yellow cup edged in red (the "pheasant's eye") — a simple, elegant flower of extraordinary fragrance used in the production of genuine Diorissimo and other fine perfumes. Unlike most daffodils, it flowers very late (often after tulips) in mid- to late spring, extending the daffodil season considerably; it is among the most shade-tolerant of daffodils and naturalizes superbly under deciduous trees. The selected form 'Actaea' is the classic large-cupped cultivar widely available.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 3–4 inches deep in fall in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; rock daffodil is a miniature species of Iberian limestone rocks requiring excellent drainage and summer drought.

directions: Plant bulbs 3–4 inches deep in fall in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, gritty soil in full sun; rock daffodil requires excellent drainage and summer drought during its dormant period — it performs best in raised beds, rock gardens, or on sharply drained slopes. Narcissus rupicola is a charming miniature wild daffodil of the Iberian Peninsula growing on limestone rocks and dry, sunny slopes; it produces slender, rush-like, blue-green leaves and small (1 inch across), bright-yellow flowers with a flat, slightly six-lobed, darker-yellow cup in mid-spring, often earlier than most daffodils. It is one of the most delicate and refined of all miniature daffodils, best appreciated up close in a rock garden, raised alpine bed, or container where its small scale can be properly seen. Excellent combined with other small bulbs such as Iris reticulata and Crocus species.

planting.note: Sow direct in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; ragged robin is an obligate of wet meadows, marshes, and stream banks and will not perform well in dry conditions.

directions: Sow direct in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; ragged robin is a plant of wet meadows, marshes, and stream margins that requires consistently moist to wet soil and will wilt and decline in dry conditions. Lychnis flos-cuculi is a native European perennial wildflower of wet meadows and marshes producing deeply and narrowly cut, feathery, rose-pink to magenta petals (each petal divided into 4 narrow, ragged lobes) on slender, branching stems in early summer — one of the most characteristically "wild" and charming of meadow flowers, excellent in naturalized wet meadow gardens alongside marsh marigold, meadow buttercup, and yellow flag iris. It self-seeds freely in wet conditions and naturalizes readily; an important plant of traditional wildflower meadows that has declined dramatically in Britain and Europe due to agricultural drainage. The white form 'White Robin' is also available.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; alpine wallflower is a perennial species from the Iberian Peninsula that is longer-lived than common wallflower (E. cheiri) in well-drained conditions.

directions: Plant rooted tip cuttings in spring in very well-drained, lean, alkaline to slightly neutral, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun; alpine wallflower requires excellent drainage and is best propagated from cuttings annually as it tends to become woody and die out in the center after 2–3 years. Erysimum linifolium is a Spanish native perennial wallflower with narrow, linear, gray-green leaves (giving it the name "flat-leaved") and produces a long, extended succession of fragrant, lavender-purple to mauve flowers in terminal clusters from spring through summer; it blooms for several months if the spent flower clusters are regularly removed. The cultivar 'Bowles' Mauve' (formerly attributed to this species but now considered a complex hybrid) is one of the longest-blooming of all perennial wallflowers, flowering nearly year-round in mild climates and making it one of the most popular of all perennial wallflowers in UK gardens.
